FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study explicitly identified: N=7514, ages 9-10 at baseline (2016-2018), 21 research sites across the U.S., 5.0 data release.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original prospective cohort analysis using modified Poisson regression and generalized structural equation modeling (GSEM) on ABCD data across Years 1-3.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in Substance Use & Misuse (2024); no indication of being a duplicate of an already-included paper.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1 hit: social media time (hours/day, Year 1) as IV paired with alcohol sipping (Year 3) as DV; Table 2 Model 1 reports RR=1.31 (95% CI 1.20-1.43), p<0.001. Alcohol sipping is an individual-trait-level substance use outcome.

FT_IC3.evidence: Multiple quantitative estimates reported: risk ratios in Table 2 (RR=1.31 for social media time -> sipping), path coefficients and indirect effects in Table 3 (B=0.011, BC 95% CI 0.007-0.016, p<0.001 for indirect effect through SMAQ).

FT_IC4.evidence: Inferential analysis performed; numeric results reported throughout Tables 2 and 3 with confidence intervals and p-values.

FT_EC7_reason: Social media time is the IV (predictor) in Models 1 and 3 predicting alcohol sipping (DV). EC7 requires SM to be DV in every analysis; here SM is IV, so EC7 does not apply.
notes: Prospective cohort design (Years 1-3). Social media time (IV) -> alcohol sipping (DV), partially mediated by SMAQ (problematic SM use). SMAQ serves only as mediator, not the primary SM variable in IC2 assessment. Clear INCLUDE.

FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study explicitly identified: N=4,718, ages 10-15 years (2018-2021), 21 demographically diverse sites, 4.0 data release.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical cross-sectional analysis of ABCD Study data using multivariable linear and logistic regression examining associations of screen time and steps with CVD risk factors.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in BMC Public Health (2024); no indication of being a duplicate of an already-included paper.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: No phone/SM-specific estimate found anywhere in main tables or supplementary. The sole exposure variable is total recreational screen time (composite). Step 2: The composite is built from television shows/movies, videos, video chat, single and multi-player video games, social media, and texting (Methods p.3). This bundle includes TV, video games, and YouTube/videos alongside social media and texting -- non-phone/non-SM items are not separately broken out. Composite fails Step 2 because it includes TV and video games without per-modality breakout.

notes: Screen time composite explicitly contains TV shows/movies, videos (YouTube), video games, video chat, social media, and texting -- a mixed bundle. No per-modality (phone/SM-specific) estimate reported anywhere. Excluded under FT-EC2.

FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study explicitly identified: N=9,538, ages 9-10 at baseline (2016-2018), 21 recruitment sites, 4.0 data release.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original longitudinal empirical analysis using mixed-effects models examining associations between baseline screen time (total and by modality) and CBCL mental health symptom outcomes at Years 1 and 2 follow-up.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in BMC Public Health (2024); no indication of being a duplicate of an already-included paper.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1 hit: Table 2 (Model 2, adjusted) reports separate numeric estimates for social media (hours/day) as IV paired with each CBCL DSM-oriented mental health symptom scale (individual-trait outcomes). Example: social media -> depressive symptoms B=0.222 (95% CI -0.002, 0.47), p=0.071; social media -> conduct symptoms B=0.222 (95% CI -0.002, 0.47). Additionally, texting B=0.35 (95% CI 0.14, 0.57), p=0.002 and video chat B=0.07 (95% CI -0.19, 0.33) are reported as separate SM/phone-adjacent modalities. Social media is a named SM platform-type variable with its own coefficient paired with mental health outcomes (individual-trait level).

FT_IC3.evidence: Multiple quantitative regression coefficients with 95% CIs reported throughout Tables 2 and 3: e.g., total screen time -> depressive symptoms B=0.10 (95% CI 0.06, 0.13), p<0.001; social media -> conduct B=0.222, p=0.071; texting -> depressive B=0.35 (95% CI 0.14, 0.57), p=0.002.

FT_IC4.evidence: Inferential longitudinal analysis with numeric results throughout Tables 2 and 3 and race/ethnicity-stratified Table 3.

FT_EC7_reason: Screen time (total and modality-specific including social media) is the IV at baseline predicting mental health outcomes (DV). SM is IV, not DV, so EC7 does not apply.
notes: Prospective longitudinal design; baseline screen time (including social media as a separately reported modality) predicts CBCL mental health symptom scores at 1- and 2-year follow-up. Social media modality has its own coefficient in adjusted models (Table 2). Clear INCLUDE.
